query: what does ICD stand for in medicine

retrieved_docs.content: Indications of ICD according to American College of Cardiology/American Heart Rhythm Society (ACC/AHRS) 2008: [7] [8] [9] [10] Class I in patients having any of the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Ventricular Fibrillation -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In 2017 the American Heart Association (AHA) update estimated the total annual burden of Out of Hospital Cardiac Arrest (OHCA) at 356,500.  At least 23% of OHCA treated by Emergency Medical Service (EMS) have VF/VT as the initial rhythm. With more than 60% of cardiovascular deaths resulting from cardiac arrest, it remains the leading cause of death worldwide. [6] Modern advances in assistive devices such as implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) have had a significant impact on these numbers. Many studies have identified VF as the most common underlying arrhythmia in patients with SCD. Among patients hospitalized with acute MI, 5% to 10% have VF or VT, and another 5% will have VF or VT within 48 hours of admission. [7] The true incidence of VF is underestimated, as individuals who suffer OHCA are not covered in most of these studies. Based on Resuscitation Outcomes Consortium (ROC) data, survival to hospital discharge for VF patients was 31.4%.

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Defibrillator -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: ICD therapy is not indicated in those who do not have a reasonable expectation of survival with an acceptable functional status for at least 1 year, even if they meet implantation criteria for ICD specified in class I, IIa, and IIb recommendations above. (Level of Evidence: C)

retrieved_docs.title: Automatic Internal Cardiac Defibrillator -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The automated internal cardiac defibrillator or shock box is the common name given to the Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD). ICD is a state-of-the-art device that treats arrhythmias specifically those of ventricular origin like ventricular tachycardia and fibrillation. It has become the first line of defense in patients who are at high risk for sudden cardiac death (SCD) and has shown consistent survival benefit in cardiac arrest survivors (SCA), in patients with Heart failure and severe systolic dysfunction (left ventricular ejection fraction-LVEF less than or equal to 35%) as well as in patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). This activity reviews the indications for an AICD and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the management of patients with ventricular arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: Automatic Internal Cardiac Defibrillator -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 2012, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved the sub-cutaneous device (S-ICD) which used sub-cutaneous leads rather than intra-cardiac leads. Recently, wearable defibrillators (WCD) have also been introduced for short-term usage.

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Defibrillator -- Equipment -- The ICD header and can
retrieved_docs.content: The ICD generator communicates with the heart through a ventricular defibrillator ± atrial and LV electrode or lead. The leads are connected to the ICD via a header. The header provides holes for inserting all aspects of the ventricular defibrillator lead and, if needed, an atrial and LV lead in specific ICD models. As in the pacemaker, set screws in the header may be tightened to fix the leads in place or loosened to allow their removal. The metal casing of the ICD generator is called the “can.” The can is similar in composition to the pacemaker's and protects the ICDs' electrical elements from fluids and many external electrical sources.

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Defibrillator -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiologist or cardiac surgeon often performs ICD implantation. However, the primary care provider often cares for these patients. These healthcare professionals should closely monitor the patients for shocks and arrhythmias. Any adverse event should be referred promptly to a cardiologist. In addition, any patient who is a survivor of cardiac arrest, has structural heart disease, Unexplained syncope, Ischemic cardiomyopathy, Non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy, LV dysfunction due to prior MI, or has Non-sustained VT should be referred to a cardiologist to determine if they are candidates for an ICD.

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Defibrillator -- Clinical Significance -- SVT/VT discrimination
retrieved_docs.content: Rate branch: Rate Branch is 1 of the discrimination methods, by simultaneous counting of both atrial and ventricular events, in case of supraventricular events the atrial events are more than ventricular events, then the device diagnoses this as atrial flutter or AF. This is an SVT, and the ICD would automatically inhibit therapy. On the other hand, if V is greater than A, that is, the ventricular rate was more rapid than the atrial rate, the ICD would categorize this as VT and advance to deliver therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Defibrillator -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: ICD therapy is not recommended in NYHA Class IV with drug-refractory congestive heart failure who are not candidates for cardiac transplantation or CRT-D. (Level of Evidence: C)

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Defibrillator -- Equipment -- The ICD generator
retrieved_docs.content: The ICD generator has the necessary elements to coordinate all pacing, sensing, and defibrillation functions. These elements include the battery and electronic circuitry (brains and capacitors). The Battery: Each ICD has a battery whose lifetime depends on how much pacing and defibrillating the device is called upon to perform. Typical longevities fall between about 4 to 7 years. The Circuitry: The ICD circuitry determines how and when both bradycardia pacing and anti-tachycardia therapies are delivered. This facilitates a wide range of programming options so that each device can be tailored to function in a manner most appropriate for each individual. The ICD capacitor(s) is (are) a critical electrical element allowing for defibrillation. Capacitors perform this task by storing energy (charging) that can then be released across the heart (defibrillation/ cardioversion).

retrieved_docs.title: Defibrillation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac defibrillation is the process of delivering a transthoracic electrical current to a patient experiencing 1 of 2 life-threatening ventricular dysrhythmias: VF or pulseless VT. Under ACLS guidelines, both conditions are treated identically. Heart disease remains the leading cause of death for any sex in the United States, with 702,880 fatalities recorded in 2022, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Nearly half of these deaths occur outside of a hospital, with three-quarters taking place in the home and half of those being unwitnessed. Among adult patients, VF is the most common cause of sudden cardiac arrest.

retrieved_docs.title: Hospice Appropriate Diagnoses -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Enrollment into hospice service is not as simple as a patient’s primary physician making a prognosis of fewer than six months to live. On the contrary, many details need to be considered and meticulously assessed, and documented for a patient to transition into hospice successfully. As with each new set of guidelines, revised standards, or updated ICD codes, there are further changes to the interpretation and guidance of hospice diagnoses and regulations. Similarly, there is a consistent rolling out of new Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) expectations regarding billing and coding.

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Defibrillator -- Clinical Significance -- Arrhythmia detection
retrieved_docs.content: Proper ICD therapy is a matter of life. Most ICDs have 3 broad detection categories: normal sinus rhythm, ventricular tachycardia, and ventricular fibrillation. These rhythms are defined by rate; the device determines based on the corresponding cycle lengths or millisecond (ms) intervals. Most ICDs now offer 3-tiered therapy to terminate the tachycardia: Anti-tachycardia pacing (ATP) Cardioversion or low-energy shocks Defibrillation or high-energy shocks. Detection is usually defined according to the ability of the ICD to detect the rate of the arrhythmia.

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Defibrillator -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: ICD therapy is not recommended for patients with incessant VT or VF. (Level of Evidence: C)

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction -- Treatment / Management -- Non-surgical interventions
retrieved_docs.content: These include implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) and cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT). Before proceeding with device therapy, patients should be treated with Guideline Directed Medical Therapy (GDMT)for at least three months and reassess the LVEF. If EF remains less than or equal to 35% with a reasonable expectation of meaningful survival for more than 1 year, the recommendation is a referral for device therapy. Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) ACC/AHA 2013 guidelines [41] recommend ICD for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death for non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy (NIDCM) or ischemic CMP (ICM) at least 40 days post-MI on chronic goal-directed medical therapy (GDMT) with either LVEF ≤ 35% and NYHA class II or III symptoms or LVEF ≤ 30% and NYHA class I symptom. ESC 2016 guidelines [42] recommend ICD for primary prevention in symptomatic HF (NYHA II-III) with LVEF ≤ 35% despite ≥ three months of GDMT in ICM (IA) or NIDCM.

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Defibrillator -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: ICD therapy is not recommended for syncope of undetermined cause in a patient without inducible ventricular tachyarrhythmias and structural heart disease. (Level of Evidence: C)

Collectively, this taxonomy delineates a detailed network of cardiac‑focused diagnostic, therapeutic, monitoring, and device‑management interventions, their precise anatomical targets, and their hierarchical organization, thereby supporting accurate documentation, procedural planning, and patient‑centered decision‑making in cardiac medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2855
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure category “Insertion; revision; replacement; removal of cardiac pacemaker or cardioverter/defibrillator” serves as an overarching classification for multiple interventions on cardiac rhythm‑management devices. Within this category, the specific action of removing pacemaker electrode leads without replacement is classified as an instance of the broader insertion‑revision‑replacement‑removal group. Likewise, the procedure of replacing a cardiac pacemaker with a dual‑chamber device is also classified under this same group. In addition, the category itself functions as a classifier for procedures that fall within its scope, thereby defining and organizing these cardiac device interventions for clinical coding, documentation, and patient‑care decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1787
retrieved_docs.content: Cardioversion by intracardiac catheter, implantation of a rate‑responsive cardiac single‑chamber device, initial implantation of a cardiac single‑chamber device, removal of a cardiac pacemaker with replacement by a single‑ chamber device (rate‑responsive), removal of pacemaker electrode leads without replacement, and replacement of a cardiac pacemaker device with a dual‑chamber device all have the heart (srdce) as their direct or indirect procedural site, indicating that each of these interventions is performed on the heart. These procedures are collectively classified under the category “Insertion; revision; replacement; removal of cardiac pacemaker or cardioverter/defibrillator,” linking each specific device‑related action to a common procedural framework. Additionally, the heart (srdce) is related to the electrocardiogram (تخطيط كهربية القلب ECG), reflecting the essential role of ECG in assessing cardiac electrical activity before, during, and after such device interventions. This integrated view highlights how various pacemaker and defibrillator implantation, revision, and extraction procedures are centered on the heart and organized within a unified classification, supporting coordinated patient management and clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2089
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure category “Insertion; revision; replacement; removal of cardiac pacemaker or cardioverter/defibrillator” serves as an overarching classification for several specific cardiac device interventions. The “Initial implantation of cardiac single‑chamber device” is classified within this category, indicating that placing a single‑chamber pacemaker is considered an insertion event under the broader group. Likewise, the “Removal of cardiac pacemaker with replacement by single‑chamber device, rate‑responsive” falls under the same classification, reflecting a procedure in which an existing pacemaker is extracted and a rate‑responsive single‑chamber device is implanted. The “Removal of pacemaker electrode leads without replacement” is also grouped within this category, representing a revision or removal action that does not involve implantation of a new device. Additionally, the “Replacement of cardiac pacemaker device with dual‑chamber device” is included, denoting a replacement procedure that upgrades to a dual‑chamber system. The classification system itself acknowledges that the “Insertion; revision; replacement; removal of cardiac pacemaker or cardioverter/defibrillator” category defines and organizes these related interventions, providing a unified framework for coding, billing, and clinical documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1719
retrieved_docs.content: Other cardiovascular procedures are classified as a subset of Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified, which in turn is placed under the parent category إجراء تدخلي. In addition, Other cardiovascular procedures are also regarded as part of other diagnostic procedures, reflecting their dual role within the medical taxonomy as both interventional and diagnostic entities. This hierarchical organization highlights how specific cardiovascular interventions are integrated into broader categories of procedures and interventions not elsewhere classified, while also being linked to diagnostic procedure groupings for comprehensive patient care and coding.

retrieved_docs.content: The procedure termed “Initial implantation of cardiac single‑chamber device” is classified as part of the broader procedural category “Insertion; revision; replacement; removal of cardiac pacemaker or cardioverter/defibrillator”.
